questions.q: A DC motor performs which energy transformation?
options: Mechanical (rotational) energy into electrical energy
Electrical energy into rotational kinetic (mechanical) energy
Chemical energy into magnetic energy
Heat energy into electrical energy
questions.explanation: A motor converts electrical energy into rotational kinetic (mechanical) energy. This is the exact inverse of a generator, which converts mechanical energy into electrical energy.

questions.q: Which list correctly names the four main components of a DC motor?
options: Rotor coil, field magnets, brushes, split-ring commutator
Rotor coil, slip rings, brushes, capacitor
Stator coil, field magnets, transformer, commutator
Armature, slip rings, brushes, diode
questions.explanation: The four parts are the rotor (armature) coil, the field magnets (stator), the brushes, and the split-ring commutator. A DC motor uses a split-ring commutator, not slip rings.

questions.q: What is the function of the split-ring commutator?
options: To supply the external magnetic field
To reverse the current in the coil every half-turn, at the zero-torque position
To increase the supply voltage
To store charge between rotations
questions.explanation: The commutator is a rotating mechanical switch that reverses the current in the coil every half-turn, exactly at the zero-torque (dead) position, so the torque always acts in the same rotational direction — converting oscillation into continuous rotation.

questions.q: Why must a DC motor use a split-ring commutator while an AC motor uses slip rings?
options: DC current never flows, so it must be switched on mechanically
DC flows in only one direction, so the reversal must be forced mechanically; an AC supply already reverses itself
AC motors do not produce torque
Slip rings reverse the current faster than a commutator
questions.explanation: A DC supply flows one way, so the current reversal in the coil must be done mechanically by the split ring. An AC supply already reverses direction on its own, so an AC motor needs only slip rings (continuous, unsplit) to maintain the connection — no reversal device.

questions.q: Why are the brushes typically made of graphite?
options: Graphite is magnetic
Graphite both conducts electricity and self-lubricates, sliding smoothly on the commutator
Graphite reverses the current
Graphite increases the supply voltage
questions.explanation: Graphite conducts electricity and self-lubricates, so the stationary brushes slide smoothly against the spinning commutator without grinding it down, maintaining the connection between the supply and the rotating coil.

questions.q: Without a commutator, what happens to a current loop at the zero-torque (dead) position?
options: It stops dead and remains stationary
Its momentum carries it past, but the unchanged forces then push it back, so it oscillates
It accelerates without limit
The current reverses on its own
questions.explanation: Inertia carries the loop past the dead point, but because the forces have not changed direction, they then produce a torque pushing it back. The result is oscillation about the dead point, not continuous rotation. (It does not simply stop — claiming it "stops" is a trap.)

questions.q: Which statement about the force and torque on the active coil sides as the coil rotates is correct?
options: Both the force and the torque are constant
The force stays constant in size, but the torque varies with orientation
The force varies, but the torque is constant
Both the force and the torque are zero at the parallel position
questions.explanation: The active sides stay perpendicular to the field, so the force stays constant at \(BIL\). The torque varies because the lever arm changes — maximum when the coil plane is parallel to the field, zero when perpendicular. Distinguishing "constant force, varying torque" is the mark.

questions.q: A coil of 20 turns and area \(5\times10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^2}\) carries 2 A in a 0.1 T field. What is the maximum torque, and at what orientation does it occur?
options: \(2\times10^{-2}\ \mathrm{N\,m}\), with the plane of the coil parallel to the field
\(2\times10^{-2}\ \mathrm{N\,m}\), with the plane of the coil perpendicular to the field
\(2\times10^{-1}\ \mathrm{N\,m}\), with the plane parallel to the field
\(4\times10^{-2}\ \mathrm{N\,m}\), with the plane perpendicular to the field
questions.explanation: Maximum torque is \(nBIA = 20 \times 0.1 \times 2 \times 5\times10^{-3} = 2\times10^{-2}\ \mathrm{N\,m}\), occurring when \(\cos\theta = 1\) — i.e. when the plane of the coil is parallel to the field.

questions.q: Apart from raising \(n\), \(B\), \(I\) or \(A\), which design feature helps increase a motor's torque?
options: Using slip rings instead of a commutator
Inserting a soft-iron (ferromagnetic) core in the armature to concentrate the field
Removing the field magnets
Reducing the number of turns
questions.explanation: A soft-iron core concentrates and strengthens the magnetic field through the coil, effectively increasing \(B\) and so the torque. Curved (radial) pole pieces and multiple coils also help by keeping torque high and smooth, but the iron core is the classic example.

questions.q: If you double the number of turns on a coil and halve the current, what happens to the torque?
options: It doubles
It halves
It stays the same
It quadruples
questions.explanation: Torque is proportional to \(n \times I\). Doubling \(n\) (\(\times 2\)) and halving \(I\) (\(\times \tfrac12\)) gives \(2 \times \tfrac12 = 1\) — no change. Reasoning from the proportionality is faster than re-substituting.
